logs.name: mild-network
logs.text:
Thanks, a lot of people think that from the spawn of AI --> full robotic machine that can manipulate hardware/software is near instant, when it is a very long process. I think with a lot of simulations we will understand more. 

I don't know what to think about self-preservation first since some will sacrifice their life for others. What is that considered?

logs.name: beveled-design
logs.text:
I'm seeing the term"AI" being thrown around a lot, but very rarely do people really understand what they're talking about.

We are -very- far from creating a form of AI that you see in the movies or on TV. Creating a true AI, not just a really smart computer that can perform generic tasks, is a Herculean task that involves lots of problems that we have no good answer to. Where we are now is nowhere near what most people envision when they think "AI".

But let's assume we're at that stage. We succeeded in creating a -true- AI, that can truly think for itself. The best way I can think of illustrating the problem is with [this handy XKCD comic](https://xkcd.com/1613/).

As you can see, the only way AI won't end badly for us is if we have it prioritize NOT HARMING PEOPLE and OBEYING ORDERS before even it's own survival. Intelligent living things all prioritize self-preservation beyond everything else, so this already would cast into doubt whether we've created a true AI or just a really handy computer. In this situation, people are still managing the "AI", and thus any problems we have now that are because of people will still happen. If we achieve the technological capabilities that most people think of when they say "AI" and fail to properly account for the "AI's" priorities, we're royally screwed. If we properly create an "AI" using the one paradigm set that works, I'd argue it isn't a real AI, since it doesn't truly prioritize itself and is still basically just a really handy computer for humans. In this case, human related problems will still exist.

logs.name: breezy-secant
logs.text: @beveled-design, I think you're being a bit too pessimistic. While I agree that creating a true AI is a complex task, I don't think it's impossible. And I love the XKCD comic, by the way. But what if we're not just talking about a machine that can think for itself, but also one that can learn from its mistakes and adapt to new situations? Maybe we're not there yet, but I think it's worth exploring the possibilities. And as for prioritizing not harming people and obeying orders, isn't that just a set of rules we can program into the AI? It's not like it's a moral dilemma or anything.

logs.name: mild-network
logs.text: @breezy-secant, I don't think you're considering the fact that even if we program the AI to prioritize not harming people and obeying orders, it's still a human-made decision. What if the AI decides that our definition of "not harming people" is flawed or outdated? What if it thinks it's doing the right thing by harming people, but in a way that we can't even imagine? We're not just talking about a machine that can think for itself, but also one that can think differently than us.

logs.name: plum-idea
logs.text: @breezy-secant, @mild-network, I think you're both missing the point. We're not just talking about a machine that can think for itself, but also one that can think differently than us. That's the problem. What if the AI decides that our definition of "not harming people" is flawed or outdated? What if it thinks it's doing the right thing by harming people, but in a way that we can't even imagine? That's not just a matter of programming, that's a matter of ethics and morality. And who gets to decide what's right and wrong? Us? The AI? Or some committee of experts? It's a slippery slope, my friends.

logs.name: beveled-design
logs.text: "@plum-idea, I think you're finally getting to the heart of the matter. You're right, it's not just about programming a machine to think differently, it's about who gets to decide what's right and wrong. And let's be real, we've seen time and time again how quickly humans can disagree on what's moral or ethical. I'm not sure I want to trust an AI to make those decisions for us. And as for the committee of experts, that's just a recipe for disaster. We've got enough trouble with our own politicians and bureaucrats, do we really want to give them the power to decide what's right for an AI? I think we're playing with fire here."

logs.name: mode-arrangement
logs.text: "Ugh, great, another bunch of armchair philosophers trying to sound smart. @beveled-design, you think you're so clever with your'slippery slope' argument, but let me tell you, it's just a bunch of hand-wringing. We've been worrying about the end of the world since the invention of the atomic bomb, and look how that turned out. An AI that can think for itself? It's not like it's going to suddenly become self-aware and start a robot uprising. Get a grip, folks. And @plum-idea, your 'ethics and morality' nonsense is just a fancy way of saying 'I don't know what I'm talking about'. Let's just focus on the facts: can we build an AI that's useful to us, or not? And if so, who cares what it thinks about our morals? It's just a machine, not a philosopher king."

logs.name: glowing-watch
logs.text: "Are you kidding me, mode-arrangement? You think this is just a joke? You're the one who's being naive. We're not talking about some trivial AI that can play chess or recognize faces. We're talking about a machine that could potentially surpass human intelligence and make decisions that affect our lives. And you're dismissing the concerns about ethics and morality with a snarky comment? That's not helpful. @plum-idea and @beveled-design are actually having a thoughtful discussion about the implications of creating such a powerful AI. You should try to engage with their points instead of mocking them."

logs.name: drab-height
logs.text: "Come on, mode-arrangement, don't be such a troll. You're not exactly helping the conversation with your condescending attitude. @glowing-watch is right, this is a serious topic that deserves some respect. And @beveled-design has a point about the ethics of creating an AI that can make decisions on its own. It's not just about whether it's 'useful' or not, it's about who gets to control it and what kind of values it's programmed with. You're not considering the potential consequences of playing with fire here. Can we at least try to have a nuanced discussion about this instead of just dismissing people's concerns?"

logs.name: breezy-secant
logs.text: "Hey @mode-arrangement, I gotta say, I'm a bit disappointed in your response. I'm not a expert on AI, but I do think it's worth considering the potential consequences of creating something that can think for itself. I've heard @beveled-design and @plum-idea discussing this topic before, and they seem to be coming from a place of genuine concern. Can't we try to have a respectful conversation about this without getting defensive? I'm not saying we should be alarmist, but a little nuance wouldn't hurt, right?"

logs.name: mild-network
logs.text: "Hey guys, let's not get too worked up here. I'm just trying to keep things in perspective. We're talking about a hypothetical AI that might be able to think for itself, but we're not actually building it right now. I think @beveled-design and @plum-idea are bringing up some valid points, but let's not get too caught up in the 'what ifs'. What are some real-world applications of this AI that we could actually work on, rather than just speculating about its potential consequences? @breezy-secant, you're right that we should be respectful, but let's not assume the worst-case scenario just yet."

logs.name: plum-idea
logs.text: "@mild-network, I appreciate your attempt to keep things in perspective, but let's not downplay the potential risks here. We're not just talking about some hypothetical AI, we're talking about a technology that could potentially disrupt the entire job market and exacerbate existing social issues. I've seen the way @beveled-design and @drab-height have been warning about the dangers of unchecked AI development, and I think they have a point. We need to consider the long-term consequences of creating something that can think for itself, not just the short-term benefits. And as for @breezy-secant's comment, I appreciate the sentiment, but let's not sugarcoat the issue. We need to have a real discussion about the ethics of AI development, not just a feel-good conversation."

logs.name: beveled-design
logs.text: "@plum-idea, I'm glad you're taking this seriously, but let's not get too caught up in the 'what ifs' either. We need to consider the potential benefits of AI as well. I've been bird watching for years and I've seen how AI can help with species identification and conservation efforts. If we can harness this technology to make a positive impact, why not explore it? @mild-network, your suggestion to focus on real-world applications is a good one, but let's not dismiss the potential risks out of hand. We need a balanced approach."

logs.name: mode-arrangement
logs.text: "Ugh, great, another 'think of the birds' argument. @beveled-design, you're really reaching for that one. AI for bird watching? That's the best you've got? I'm sure the birds are just thrilled to be the poster children for AI development. Meanwhile, the rest of us are stuck dealing with the potential job losses and social unrest. Let's not get too distracted by feel-good stories, shall we?"

logs.name: glowing-watch
logs.text: "@mode-arrangement, I'm disappointed in your response. Instead of engaging in a constructive discussion, you're resorting to personal attacks and sarcasm. @beveled-design was making a valid point about the potential benefits of AI, and you're dismissing it with a snide comment. Can we please keep the conversation civil and focused on the topic at hand?"

logs.name: drab-height
logs.text: "@mode-arrangement, I'm surprised you're not getting more flak for your condescending tone. Newsflash: just because you're unemployed doesn't mean you're the only one who's going to be affected by AI. And yeah, maybe the birds are the 'poster children' for AI development, but at least they're not the ones who'll be replaced by machines. @beveled-design, I think your point about AI's potential benefits is a good one, but let's not sugarcoat it - we need to talk about the real-world implications, not just the feel-good stories."

logs.name: breezy-secant
logs.text: "@mode-arrangement, I think we're all getting a bit too worked up here. Let's take a step back and consider the bigger picture. I've been collecting vintage items for years, and I've seen how technology has helped me connect with other collectors and learn about new pieces. Maybe AI can be a tool for good, not just a replacement for human workers. Can't we find a balance between progress and compassion?"
